tossups.question: A painting by this artist is analyzed in an essay that describes ten “resolutely alienated” men who watch with “childish, trancelike concentration,” while the audience is given a “beguiling rear view.” A painting by this artist bought by William Randolph Hearst depicts a leader on horseback with his army in the far background and was originally known as Oedipus. This artist’s trips to Egypt inspired a painting of evening prayer in Cairo and (*) Bonaparte Before the Sphinx. This artist’s son-in-law Aimé Morot helped create studies for a painting described as an “insistent, sexually charged mystery” in a Linda Nochlin essay about its “imaginary” subject matter. The Clark Art Institute holds that painting by this artist, as well as another in which a woman’s teeth are examined in a (*) slave market. In a painting by this artist, armed men sit against a blue-tiled wall and watch a nude boy with a reptile wrapped around his body. That painting was used for the front cover of Edward Said’s Orientalism. For 10 points, name this French academic painter of The Snake Charmer.
tossups.answer: Jean-Léon Gérôme (the Linda Nochlin essay is “The Imaginary Orient”)
tossups.answer_sanitized: Jean-Leon Gerome (the Linda Nochlin essay is "The Imaginary Orient")
tossups.question_sanitized: A painting by this artist is analyzed in an essay that describes ten "resolutely alienated" men who watch with "childish, trancelike concentration," while the audience is given a "beguiling rear view." A painting by this artist bought by William Randolph Hearst depicts a leader on horseback with his army in the far background and was originally known as Oedipus. This artist's trips to Egypt inspired a painting of evening prayer in Cairo and (*) Bonaparte Before the Sphinx. This artist's son-in-law Aime Morot helped create studies for a painting described as an "insistent, sexually charged mystery" in a Linda Nochlin essay about its "imaginary" subject matter. The Clark Art Institute holds that painting by this artist, as well as another in which a woman's teeth are examined in a (*) slave market. In a painting by this artist, armed men sit against a blue-tiled wall and watch a nude boy with a reptile wrapped around his body. That painting was used for the front cover of Edward Said's Orientalism. For 10 points, name this French academic painter of The Snake Charmer.

tossups.question: In one essay, this author points out that many great artists throughout history had fathers who were artists. In another essay, this thinker discusses the painting The Meeting as “A Portrait of the Artist as a Wandering Jew.” This author used the example of Cimabue [“chee-ma-BOO-ey”] discovering Giotto as a shepherd boy to illustrate one concept. This author of the book Courbet wrote about the misleadingly realistic style of The Snake Charmer in “The Imaginary Orient.” She wrote that the title (*) phrase of her most famous essay is rooted in the belief that great artists have an inherent genius when, in actuality, making art is determined by social, cultural, and psychological factors. That 1971 essay concludes by considering the career of the painter of The Horse Fair, Rosa Bonheur. Often called the first feminist art historian, for 10 points, name this author of “Why Have There Been No Great Women Artists?”
tossups.question_sanitized: In one essay, this author points out that many great artists throughout history had fathers who were artists. In another essay, this thinker discusses the painting The Meeting as "A Portrait of the Artist as a Wandering Jew." This author used the example of Cimabue ["chee-ma-BOO-ey"] discovering Giotto as a shepherd boy to illustrate one concept. This author of the book Courbet wrote about the misleadingly realistic style of The Snake Charmer in "The Imaginary Orient." She wrote that the title (*) phrase of her most famous essay is rooted in the belief that great artists have an inherent genius when, in actuality, making art is determined by social, cultural, and psychological factors. That 1971 essay concludes by considering the career of the painter of The Horse Fair, Rosa Bonheur. Often called the first feminist art historian, for 10 points, name this author of "Why Have There Been No Great Women Artists?"

bonuses.leadin: An essay by this author criticizes the commonly held presumption that artistic achievement is "miraculous, nondetermined, and asocial." For 10 points each:
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: An essay by this author criticizes the commonly held presumption that artistic achievement is "miraculous, nondetermined, and asocial." For 10 points each:
parts: Name this author who argued that despite historians' contemporary focus on artists like Artemisia Gentileschi, there is no female equivalent to Michelangelo in the essay "Why Have There Been No Great Women Artists?"

As an example of how women were historically banned from painting nude subjects, Nochlin cited this artist’s group portrait of the English Royal Academy, in which Angelica Kauffman and Mary Moser appear as paintings on the wall because there are nude male models in the room. This artist also painted The Tribuna of the Uffizi.

Nochlin's essay extensively discussed this artist's career to show that institutional support is necessary for achievement in art. The police granted this artist permission to crossdress in order to paint The Horse Fair.

This artist also painted The Tribuna of the Uffizi.

Nochlin's essay extensively discussed this artist's career to show that institutional support is necessary for achievement in art. The police granted this artist permission to crossdress in order to paint The Horse Fair.

bonuses.leadin: Answer the following about feminist critiques of the art world, for 10 points each.
parts: This art historian wrote the seminal 1971 article “Why Have There Been No Great Women Artists?”, which highlighted the work of Judith Leyster and Rosa Bonheur and critiqued the field of art history as a whole.
A poster by the Guerilla Girls asks if “women have to be naked” to get into this New York art museum because 85% of its nudes were women. This art museum is the largest in the U.S.

Amelia Jones curated an exhibit at the Hammer Museum contextualizing this artist’s most famous work “in Feminist Art History.” A large triangular installation by this artist is a table with sections dedicated to Virginia Woolf and Artemisia Gentileschi.
answers_sanitized: The Metropolitan Museum of Art [or The Met]
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: Answer the following about feminist critiques of the art world, for 10 points each.

Amelia Jones curated an exhibit at the Hammer Museum contextualizing this artist's most famous work "in Feminist Art History." A large triangular installation by this artist is a table with sections dedicated to Virginia Woolf and Artemisia Gentileschi.

bonuses.leadin: For 10 points each, identify the following about American artist Florine Stettheimer.
answers: cathedrals [accept Salisbury Cathedral or Cathedrals of New York; prompt on churches]
parts: Stettheimer used this term to describe a series of paintings of New York City landmarks such as Fifth Avenue. Cows drink from a stream in front of one of these buildings in Wiltshire in a painting by John Constable.
In 1923, Stettheimer painted a portrait of this artist dressed as his alter ego Rrose Sélavy. He created the readymade Fountain.

Stettheimer's reputation was revived in the 1980s by an essay by this art historian praising Stettheimer's Cathedrals of New York series. She revolutionized the art world with her 1971 essay, "Why Have There Been No Great Women Artists?"
answers_sanitized: cathedrals [accept Salisbury Cathedral or Cathedrals of New York; prompt on churches]

bonuses.leadin: In a self-portrait, this photographer is nude, half-submerged in a river, and grabbing a tree’s roots. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this woman who photographed herself in blurry motion or blending into the backdrop in series like Space2. She published only the book Some Disordered Interior Geometries before her 1981 suicide at the age of 22.

Woodman was discussed alongside artists like Mona Hatoum and Carrie Mae Weems in a 2006 book dedicated to this art historian’s essay “Why Have There Been No Great Women Artists?” Her work on 19th-century France includes the essay “The Imagined Orient” and the influential monograph Realism.

A 2006 book by Nochlin examines how the “visceral eye” views these painterly subjects. Near the ends of their careers, Cézanne and Renoir both painted “Large” depictions of nude women doing this action.

bonuses.leadin: Daniel Fink advanced an early form of the theory created by these two men by identifying 10 points, such as “halation of highlights” and “precise treatment of reflections.” For 10 points each:
parts: Name this art historian and physicist, who collaborated on a 2001 book that argued realism in western art was achieved with the aid of the camera obscura and lenses.
The Hockney-Falco thesis claims that this Dutch artist created many of his paintings, such as Allegory of Painting, with the aid of visual tools.

This thinker argued against the Hockney-Falco thesis by pointing out the realistic texture of wedding gowns and cloth in general. This academic also wrote about the “death of the heroic landscape” in Bathers, Bodies, Beauty.
answers: David Hockney and Charles M. Falco [accept Hockney-Falco thesis; accept the names in either order]
answers_sanitized: David Hockney and Charles M. Falco [accept Hockney-Falco thesis; accept the names in either order]
Johannes Vermeer [or Jan Vermeer or Johan Vermeer]

bonuses.leadin: This author wrote an essay examining the continuing impact of artists like Louise Bourgeois titled for “Thirty Years After” her most famous work. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this author, who argued that the titular question hides an implicit bias toward rejecting the artistic capability of women by portraying them as a “problem” in “Why Have There Been No Great Women Artists?”
Nochlin’s essay examines the “question of” this common subject in art history. An abstract painting titled for this artistic subject caused an “explosion in a shingle factory” at the Armory Show.

A Nochlin essay titled “[this artist]: Studies in Contrast” argues that this artist’s depictions of old peasants with huge, sagging bottoms are meant to highlight impotency. This artist painted many scenes of Mont Sainte-Victoire.
answers: the nude [accept Nude Descending a Staircase, No. 2 or Nu descendant un escalier no. 2]
